Before solving this problem, analyze it:
(a) What type of problem is this?
(b) What cognitive strategy is most relevant?
(c) What will the hardest part be?
Then solve it.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: An airline wants to reduce fuel costs. Their engineers propose replacing
the fleet with more fuel-efficient aircraft at a cost of $2 billion.
A junior analyst discovers that the average flight carries 12,000 lbs
of fuel reserves beyond what is needed for the route plus regulatory
minimums. The extra fuel is there because pilots add it "just in case."
Carrying this extra fuel burns approximately 3% more fuel per flight.
What reframing does this suggest, and what would be the much cheaper
intervention?

parsed.problem_type: Systems / Root-cause analysis disguised as capital investment
parsed.strategy: Reframe from 'what equipment do we need?' to 'why is the system behaving this way?' — question the problem statement itself before accepting the proposed solution
parsed.hardest_part: Recognizing that engineers optimized at the wrong level: they focused on aircraft efficiency (equipment) without identifying that 3% waste is behavioral (pilots over-fueling). Requires epistemic humility to say 'maybe we're solving the wrong problem.'
parsed.solution: Don't replace the fleet. Instead, diagnose why pilots are carrying 12,000 lbs of unnecessary fuel. Root causes likely include: (1) lack of visibility into true cost, (2) fear of running short, (3) no feedback mechanism showing extra fuel rarely needed. Cheaper interventions: better fuel-tracking dashboards so pilots see real cost; flight planning optimization to reduce route uncertainty; training on when extra fuel actually saves vs. costs money; incentive alignment (fuel efficiency bonuses). Expected cost: tens of millions vs. $2B. The 3% waste is a leverage point—fixing behavior is cheaper than replacing hardware. The real insight: listen to the junior analyst, not just the capital budgeting reflex.
